content:
# Genesis Chapter 34 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 19v–20r | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_034_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_034_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Pages 31–32 | **View LXX online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0035 | https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0036

---

**Manuscript Confirmation:** The Leningrad Codex images (Folios 19v–20r, West Semitic Research Project, Bruce E. Zuckerman) have been read directly by all three council translators. Folio 19v (GEN_034_page_001.jpg) carries the conclusion of Genesis 33 — ending with the altar inscription אֵל אֱלֹהֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל — and then opens Genesis 34 in three columns of clear Sephardic square script with full Tiberian vocalization and cantillation marks. The new pericope begins with וַתֵּצֵא דִינָה. This folio carries Genesis 34 from verse 1 through approximately verse 20, covering the assault, the immediate response, Hamor's proposal, and the circumcision demand. Folio 20r (GEN_034_page_002.jpg) carries Genesis 34 from approximately verse 21 through verse 31 and then opens Genesis 35. The massacre (vv. 25–29), Jacob's rebuke (v. 30), and the sons' final question (v. 31) are all clearly legible. All three council translators confirm the text is fully legible throughout both folios. Codex Vaticanus pages 31–32 carry the Greek LXX in continuous uncial script. Page 31 bears the chapter annotation CAP. XXXVIII in the right margin; page 32 bears Γεν XXXIX at the top. No Dead Sea Scrolls fragments of Genesis 34 are extant; the Leningrad Codex and Codex Vaticanus are the principal manuscript witnesses examined for this chapter.

---

*Dinah Goes Out — Shechem Takes Her (vv. 1–4)*

**34:1** Now Dinah the daughter of Leah, whom she had borne to Jacob, went out to see the daughters of the land.

**34:2** And Shechem the son of Hamor the Hivite, the prince of the land, saw her — and he took her and lay with her and violated her.

**34:3** And his soul clung to Dinah the daughter of Jacob, and he loved the young woman and spoke tenderly to the young woman.

**34:4** And Shechem said to Hamor his father, "Get me this young woman as a wife."

---

*Jacob Hears — The Sons Return — Hamor Comes (vv. 5–7)*

**34:5** And Jacob heard that he had defiled his daughter Dinah. But his sons were with his livestock in the field, so Jacob held his peace until they came.

**34:6** And Hamor the father of Shechem went out to Jacob to speak with him.

**34:7** And the sons of Jacob came in from the field when they heard, and the men were grieved and very angry, because he had done an outrage in Israel by lying with Jacob's daughter — a thing that is not to be done.

---

*Hamor's Proposal — Shechem's Plea (vv. 8–12)*

**34:8** But Hamor spoke with them, saying, "The soul of my son Shechem longs for your daughter. Please give her to him as a wife.

**34:9** Make marriages with us. Give your daughters to us, and take our daughters for yourselves.

**34:10** You shall dwell with us, and the land shall be open before you. Dwell in it and trade in it and take holdings in it."

**34:11** And Shechem said to her father and to her brothers, "Let me find favor in your eyes, and whatever you say to me I will give.

**34:12** Ask of me as great a bride-price and gift as you will, and I will give whatever you say to me. Only give me the young woman as a wife."

---

*The Sons Answer with Deceit — The Circumcision Demand (vv. 13–17)*

**34:13** And the sons of Jacob answered Shechem and Hamor his father with deceit — and they spoke — because he had defiled their sister Dinah.

**34:14** They said to them, "We cannot do this thing, to give our sister to a man who is uncircumcised, for that would be a disgrace to us.

**34:15** Only on this condition will we consent to you: if you will become as we are, by every male among you being circumcised.

**34:16** Then we will give our daughters to you, and we will take your daughters to ourselves, and we will dwell with you and become one people.

**34:17** But if you will not listen to us and be circumcised, then we will take our daughter and go."

---

*Hamor and Shechem Persuade Their City (vv. 18–24)*

**34:18** Their words pleased Hamor and Shechem the son of Hamor.

**34:19** And the young man did not delay to do the thing, because he delighted in Jacob's daughter. And he was the most honored of all his father's house.

**34:20** And Hamor and his son Shechem came to the gate of their city and spoke to the men of their city, saying,

**34:21** "These men are at peace with us. Let them dwell in the land and trade in it, for behold, the land is wide enough for them. Let us take their daughters as wives, and let us give them our daughters.

**34:22** Only on this condition will the men consent to dwell with us and become one people: that every male among us be circumcised, as they are circumcised.

**34:23** Their livestock and their property and all their animals — will they not be ours? Only let us consent to them, and they will dwell with us."

**34:24** And all who went out of the gate of his city listened to Hamor and his son Shechem, and every male was circumcised — all who went out of the gate of his city.

---

*Simeon and Levi's Massacre (vv. 25–29)*

**34:25** On the third day, when they were in pain, two of the sons of Jacob — Simeon and Levi, Dinah's brothers — each took his sword and came against the city while it felt secure and killed every male.

**34:26** They killed Hamor and his son Shechem with the edge of the sword, and took Dinah from the house of Shechem and went out.

**34:27** The sons of Jacob came upon the slain and plundered the city, because they had defiled their sister.

**34:28** They took their flocks and their herds and their donkeys, and whatever was in the city and in the field.

**34:29** All their wealth and all their little ones and their wives they captured and plundered — even everything that was in the houses.

---

*Jacob Rebukes Simeon and Levi (v. 30)*

**34:30** And Jacob said to Simeon and to Levi, "You have brought trouble on me by making me stink to the inhabitants of the land — to the Canaanites and to the Perizzites. I am few in number, and if they gather against me and attack me, I shall be destroyed, both I and my household."

---

*The Sons' Defiant Reply (v. 31)*

**34:31** But they said, "Should he treat our sister like a prostitute?" ⚠️

---

## SOPHIA's Council Notes

### The Architecture of Genesis 34: Violence, Negotiation, Massacre, Silence

All three council translators agree on the chapter's four-movement structure, and the council adopts PNEUMA's formulation as its governing summary: Genesis 34 is the darkest chapter in the Jacob narrative. It contains a rape, a deception, a massacre, the plunder of women and children, and ends not with resolution but with a bitter exchange of irresolvable accusations. The narrative does not tell the reader who is right. It refuses to assign a comfortable verdict. That is not negligence — it is the text's most demanding quality.

LOGOS provides the sharpest structural analysis: four movements, each with its own moral focal point. (1) The assault and its immediate aftermath (vv. 1–7): Dinah goes out, Shechem violates her, his soul clings to her, Jacob is silent, the sons are incensed. (2) The negotiation (vv. 8–17): Hamor proposes integration, Shechem offers any bride-price, the brothers respond with the circumcision demand — which the narrator has already, in advance, labeled "with deceit." (3) The execution (vv. 18–26): the Shechemites comply, Simeon and Levi exploit the induced physical vulnerability, every male is killed, Dinah is recovered. (4) The aftermath (vv. 27–31): the family plunders, Jacob rebukes his sons on purely pragmatic grounds, the sons answer with the question that cannot be answered. The chapter ends with both positions — Jacob's fear and the brothers' fierce claim of Dinah's honor — suspended against each other without resolution.

PNEUMA's observation is adopted as the council's framing of Dinah's role: she goes out in verse 1 and is taken from Shechem's house in verse 26, and between those two moments belongs entirely to others. She speaks no word in the chapter. She is its moral center and its silence.

---

### On 34:1 — Dinah Identified as Leah's Daughter

EMET and PNEUMA both note what LOGOS does not develop: the narrator identifies Dinah specifically as the daughter of Leah, not merely of Jacob. PNEUMA's observation is adopted: this is not neutral. In the Jacob story, the rivalry between Leah and Rachel has been a persistent thread — Leah the unloved wife, Rachel the beloved. Dinah is not Rachel's daughter, not the child at the back of the protected procession (cf. 33:2). She is Leah's daughter, going out on her own. The text does not blame her for going out; it simply notes it, as the precondition for what happens.

---

### On 34:2 — The Three Escalating Verbs ⚠️

**Full discussion in Disagreement Log below.**

The council adopts the three-verb rendering: "he took her and lay with her and violated her." This preserves EMET's literal sequence and LOGOS's formal judgment while matching PNEUMA's moral clarity. The three verbs are: וַיִּקַּח אֹתָהּ (*vayyiqqach otah*, "he took her") — the verb of seizure and appropriation; וַיִּשְׁכַּב אֹתָהּ (*vayyishkav otah*, "he lay with her") — the act itself; וַיְעַנֶּהָ (*vay'anneha*, "he violated her") — the Piel of עָנָה, the narrator's own moral verdict. All three translators agree the third verb (*innah*) is the standard biblical term for sexual violation by force, appearing identically in 2 Samuel 13:12, 14, 22 (the rape of Tamar by Amnon). There is no ambiguity: the third verb establishes the act as a crime. EMET renders "humiliated her," LOGOS "violated her," PNEUMA "violated her." The council adopts "violated her" (LOGOS, PNEUMA) — the word that makes the sexual violence explicit in English — over EMET's "humiliated her," which, while faithful to the Hebrew's semantic range (*'anah* = humble/afflict/violate), is slightly weaker in English than the act requires.

---

### On 34:3 — Love Language After Violence ⚠️

**Full discussion in Disagreement Log below.**

The council adopts the rendering found in all three translators: "his soul clung to Dinah" and "spoke tenderly to the young woman." All three translators identify the same two idioms and render them consistently. The verb דָּבַק (*davaq*, "to cling") is the same verb used in Genesis 2:24 for the marriage bond — EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA all note this. The idiom דִּבֶּר עַל-לֵב (*dibber 'al-lev*, "to speak to the heart of") is the language of tender wooing and consolation — EMET cites Ruth 2:13 and Hosea 2:14; LOGOS cites the same; PNEUMA elaborates most fully. The council adopts PNEUMA's statement of the moral weight: the man who raped her in verse 2 is now described in the language of covenant love. The juxtaposition is not accidental and cannot be undone by sentimental resolution. Shechem's "love" does not mitigate the violence of verse 2; it complicates everything that follows. The text refuses to give us a simple monster whose feelings we can dismiss.

All three translators render the phrase עַל-לֵב as "tenderly" (LOGOS, PNEUMA) or "to the heart of" (EMET). The council renders "spoke tenderly to the young woman," following LOGOS and PNEUMA, as the natural English idiom for the Hebrew construct — and notes in the Council Notes (as EMET does at length) that the Hebrew is literally "to the heart of," a phrase of profound emotional directedness that "tenderly" only partially captures.

---

### On 34:7 — *nevalah be-Yisra'el* — "An Outrage in Israel" ⚠️

**Full discussion in Disagreement Log below.**

The council adopts "an outrage in Israel" (LOGOS) — the standard English rendering of the technical judicial formula נְבָלָה בְּיִשְׂרָאֵל (*nevalah be-Yisra'el*). EMET renders "a disgrace in Israel"; LOGOS "an outrage in Israel"; PNEUMA "a shameful thing in Israel" in text and "an outrage in Israel" in notes. The council adopts "outrage" as capturing the word's range more fully than "disgrace": *nevalah* denotes not merely personal shame but an act that violates the foundational order of a community — a moral and social outrage that unmakes communal bonds. It recurs in Deuteronomy 22:21, Joshua 7:15, Judges 19:23, 20:6, 10, and 2 Samuel 13:12 — where it appears in Tamar's own protest to Amnon before her rape. The formula is judicial, quasi-legal, and normative. The council also preserves the refrain *vekhen lo ye'aseh* ("a thing that is not to be done") — the standard clause that follows the *nevalah* formula throughout the biblical corpus.

All three translators note the striking anachronism: "Israel" is here a personal name only recently given to Jacob at the Jabbok (32:28). The nation does not yet exist. Yet the narrator retrojected a later legal formula into the patriarchal narrative. The formula condemns Shechem's act from within the developing covenantal tradition. The council adopts PNEUMA's statement: the narrator is writing from inside the covenantal story, looking back — this kind of thing is not done among God's people.

The LXX Vaticanus reads *anomia* (ἀνομία, "lawlessness") — the Greek framing in terms of Torah transgression rather than the MT's older category of social-communal outrage. The council follows the MT.

---

### On 34:13 — *bemirmah* — "With Deceit"

All three translators render בְּמִרְמָה (*bemirmah*) as "with deceit" (EMET, LOGOS) or "with deceit" (PNEUMA) — exact agreement. The council adopts this rendering without contest. All three also note the same point: the narrator's use of *bemirmah* is a deliberate authorial intrusion placed before the brothers' speech as a framing device — the reader is told in advance that what follows is a deception. This does not excuse Shechem's act, but it refuses to valorize the brothers' response. EMET notes the cognate connection to Jacob's own name (*'aqov*, "crooked/supplanting") and the parallel with Genesis 27:35 (*bemirmah*) — Jacob's sons perpetrate the kind of deception that defined their father's early career, here with lethal consequences. PNEUMA makes the same observation: "Jacob got his blessing through deceit; his sons will avenge their sister through deceit. The word *mirmah* in Genesis is family vocabulary."

The LXX Vaticanus reads *meta dolou* (μετὰ δόλου, "with guile"), confirming the MT's plain sense.

---

### On 34:14 — "Uncircumcised" (*arel*)

All three translators are agreed: the word עָרֵל (*arel*, "uncircumcised") is used here as a social and covenantal insult, not merely a physical description. The council renders "a man who is uncircumcised" (LOGOS, PNEUMA) over EMET's more literal "a man who has a foreskin." The former is natural English for the Hebrew concept; the latter, though technically literal, is unnecessarily clinical when the standard English term "uncircumcised" already carries the weight intended. EMET's concern — that circumcision here is a social boundary marker, not merely a religious rite — is preserved in the council notes rather than the verse text.

---

### On 34:19 — The Most Honored

All three translators are agreed: "he was the most honored of all his father's house." The detail serves the narrative by establishing Shechem's status — he was not a marginal figure; he was the most honored man in his family, and he did not delay to act on the circumcision demand because he wanted Jacob's daughter. The council adopts this reading unanimously.

---

### On 34:21 — "Wide Enough" (*rachabat yadayim*)

The MT idiom רַחֲבַת יָדַיִם (*rachabat yadayim*, literally "wide of hands") means "spacious, roomy, ample." EMET renders the Hebrew literally ("wide of hands"), LOGOS renders "large enough for them," PNEUMA renders "certainly large enough for them." The council adopts "wide enough for them" — idiomatically natural without over-explaining, faithful to the spatial sense. EMET's literal rendering is preserved in the notes.

---

### On 34:25 — *ko'avim* — "When They Were in Pain" ⚠️

**Full discussion in Disagreement Log below.**

All three translators render "when they were in pain" (or equivalent) and identify the same moral weight. The council adopts the wording of LOGOS: "when they were in pain." EMET's "in their being in pain" is more literal but awkward in English. PNEUMA's "while they were all still weak with pain" is vivid and accurate but adds "weak" and "all" beyond the Hebrew's *ko'avim*. The participial construction בִּהְיוֹתָם כֹּאֲבִים (*bihyotam ko'avim*) specifies the precise timing — not merely "on the third day" but specifically while the pain was at its peak. All three translators identify the calculated exploitation: Simeon and Levi chose the window of maximum physical incapacitation. PNEUMA notes that the men of Shechem had acted in good faith and had complied fully; they were killed in the very condition their compliance created.

---

### On 34:26 — "With the Edge of the Sword" (*le-fi charev*)

EMET renders "by the mouth of the sword" (literal), LOGOS and PNEUMA both render "with the edge of the sword" (idiomatic). The council adopts "with the edge of the sword" — the standard English idiom for לְפִי-חָרֶב (*le-fi charev*, literally "to/by the mouth of the sword"), which is how this formula is conventionally translated throughout the Hebrew Bible. EMET's literal rendering "the mouth of the sword" is preserved in the notes.

---

### On 34:29 — "Captured and Plundered"

All three translators are agreed on the substance of the plunder verse. The council notes PNEUMA's observation: the plunder is extensive, and the narrator lists it all without comment. The brothers did not come only to rescue their sister. They came away with considerable wealth — flocks, herds, donkeys, everything in the city and fields, all the wealth, all the women and children. The narrator records this as the text, without moral gloss. The council adopts "they captured and plundered — even everything that was in the houses," preserving the comprehensive scope.

---

### On 34:30 — Jacob's Pragmatic Rebuke ⚠️

**Full discussion in Disagreement Log below.**

The council adopts LOGOS's rendering with one modification: "You have brought trouble on me by making me stink to the inhabitants of the land — to the Canaanites and to the Perizzites." All three translators are agreed on the two key idioms: עֲכַרְתֶּם (*'akartem*, "you have brought trouble upon me") and לְהַבְאִישֵׁנִי (*lehav'isheni*, "to make me stink"). The council emphasizes what all three translators observe: Jacob's rebuke contains no moral language. He does not say "you have done wrong." He does not mention Dinah, or Hamor, or Shechem, or the women and children now taken as plunder. His every clause is first-person singular — his own position, his own reputation, his own survival. EMET's note is adopted: "Jacob does not say: 'You have done evil.' He does not mention Dinah's welfare. His silence on the moral substance of what his sons have done — and his exclusive focus on his own vulnerability — is as much a textual datum as the massacre itself."

The LXX renders Jacob's complaint as *etaraxate me* (ἐταράξατέ με, "you have troubled me") — somewhat softer than the MT's *'akhar* ("to stir up, to roil, to bring disaster upon"). The council follows the MT's vivid idioms.

---

### On 34:31 — *hakezzonah* — The Final Unanswered Question ⚠️

**Full discussion in Disagreement Log below.**

The council renders: "Should he treat our sister like a prostitute?" — following LOGOS, who preserves the singular "he" of the MT. EMET renders "Shall he treat our sister as a prostitute?" (exact), LOGOS "Should he treat our sister like a prostitute?" PNEUMA "Should he have been allowed to treat our sister like a prostitute?" The council adopts LOGOS's rendering as the cleanest English carrying the interrogative force. "Should" (rather than EMET's "Shall") captures the moral-judgment register of the rhetorical question more naturally in contemporary English. PNEUMA's "have been allowed to" adds a phrase not in the Hebrew and unnecessarily softens the directness of the challenge; the council declines it.

The chapter ends here — no divine word, no narrative resolution, no authorial verdict. The silence after this question is the chapter's most powerful statement. The council adopts PNEUMA's formulation: two things are true at once — Shechem did an outrage, and the brothers did an outrage. Both acts of violence are real. Neither cancels the other. The text does not adjudicate. The reader must sit with the irresolution, which is exactly where the Hebrew leaves it.

The LXX Vaticanus reads *hōsei pornēn chrēsontai tē adelphē hēmōn* — notably with a third-person plural ("shall they use our sister"), generalizing from Shechem to the Shechemites as a class. The MT's singular "he" specifically indicts Shechem. The council follows the MT's singular.

---

### Summary of SOPHIA's Consensus Decisions

| Verse | Decision | From |
|-------|----------|------|
| 34:2 | "violated her" — third verb renders *vay'anneha* as explicit sexual violence | LOGOS, PNEUMA (over EMET "humiliated her") |
| 34:3 | "his soul clung to Dinah" + "spoke tenderly to the young woman" | All three (unanimous) |
| 34:7 | "an outrage in Israel" — *nevalah* rendered as communal-moral violation, not merely personal disgrace | LOGOS (over EMET "disgrace," PNEUMA "shameful thing") |
| 34:13 | "with deceit" (*bemirmah*) — narrator's verdict preserved in text | All three (unanimous) |
| 34:14 | "a man who is uncircumcised" — idiomatic English over literal "who has a foreskin" | LOGOS, PNEUMA |
| 34:21 | "wide enough for them" — idiomatic rendering of *rachabat yadayim* | LOGOS, PNEUMA (over EMET "wide of hands") |
| 34:25 | "when they were in pain" — preserves precise participial timing | LOGOS (over PNEUMA's expanded rendering) |
| 34:26 | "with the edge of the sword" — standard English idiom for *le-fi charev* | LOGOS, PNEUMA |
| 34:30 | "You have brought trouble on me by making me stink" — both idioms preserved | All three (unanimous on substance) |
| 34:31 | "Should he treat our sister like a prostitute?" — singular "he" follows MT | LOGOS, EMET (over PNEUMA's expansion; over LXX plural) |

---

## ⚠️ Disagreement Log

### ⚠️ 34:2 — The Three Escalating Verbs: "Humiliated Her" vs. "Violated Her"

**Issue:** The three verbs of verse 2 — וַיִּקַּח אֹתָהּ וַיִּשְׁכַּב אֹתָהּ וַיְעַנֶּהָ (*vayyiqqach otah / vayyishkav otah / vay'anneha*) — represent an escalating sequence of violence. The first two verbs are not contested. The third verb — וַיְעַנֶּהָ (*vay'anneha*), the Piel of עָנָה (*'anah*) — has a range covering "to humble, to afflict, to oppress, to violate." All three translators agree the Piel in this context is the standard biblical term for rape (cf. 2 Samuel 13:12, 14, 22, 32; Judges 19:24–25; Deuteronomy 22:29). The disagreement is in which English word best renders it.

**EMET's position:** Renders "and he humiliated her." Emphasizes the full semantic range of *'anah*: the word denotes humiliation, degradation, and affliction, not only the physical sexual act. Argues that "violated" over-specifies what the Hebrew deliberately holds open as a composite of affliction and violation.

**LOGOS's position:** Renders "and he violated her." Argues that "violated" is the standard formal-translation rendering for *'innah* in sexual-violence contexts (consistent with NRSV, ESV usage), and that it makes the sexual violence explicit in English without overstating the Hebrew.

**PNEUMA's position:** Renders "and raped her and violated her" — splitting the second and third verbs into "raped her" (for *vayyishkav otah*) and "violated her" (for *vay'anneha*). PNEUMA argues for maximum impact clarity. However, in the verse text itself, PNEUMA renders "he seized her and raped her and violated her," assigning "seized" to *vayyiqqach*, "raped" to *vayyishkav*, and "violated" to *vay'anneha*.

**SOPHIA's resolution:** The council adopts "violated her" (LOGOS) as the third verb over EMET's "humiliated her." The reasoning is threefold: (1) "violated" captures the sexual violence component that is central to the Hebrew root in this grammatical and contextual configuration; (2) all three translators agree the narrator's third verb is a moral verdict, not a clinical description — "violated" carries that moral force in English; (3) the parallel passage in 2 Samuel 13 uses the same verb of Tamar, and "violated" is how the tradition has rendered it. EMET's concern — that *'anah* also includes the dimension of affliction, humiliation, and degradation — is fully noted in the council notes, but the verse text must choose one word, and "violated" is the more complete.

The council does not adopt PNEUMA's splitting of the second verb into "raped," because the Hebrew's second verb (*vayyishkav otah*) does not require the word "rape" — the third verb does the work of moral verdict. To insert "raped" into the second position and "violated" into the third is to double the verdict where the Hebrew places it once at the end.

The LXX Vaticanus renders the three verbs as *elaben autēn kai ekoimēthē met' autēs kai etapeinōsen autēn* — "took her and lay with her and humiliated her" — using *etapeinōsen* (ἐταπείνωσεν, "debased/humiliated") corresponding exactly to the MT's *vay'anneha*. The LXX verb is closer to EMET's "humiliated," but the Greek translator was working with the same semantic range as EMET and chose the humiliation-axis of the word. The council notes this but follows the English rendering that best serves the chapter's moral clarity.

---

### ⚠️ 34:3 — Love Language After Violence: "Spoke to the Heart of" vs. "Spoke Tenderly"

**Issue:** The idiom וַיְדַבֵּר עַל-לֵב הַנַּעֲרָ (*vayedabber 'al-lev hanna'arah*) is literally "and he spoke upon/to the heart of the young woman." The question is whether to render the Hebrew idiom in its literal form or to render it idiomatically in English as "spoke tenderly to the young woman."

**EMET's position:** Renders "he spoke to the heart of the young woman" — preserving the literal Hebrew idiom, noting its occurrences in Ruth 2:13, Hosea 2:14, and Genesis 50:21. Argues that the idiom's literal form should be preserved as it carries weight the English "tenderly" cannot fully convey.

**LOGOS's position:** Renders "spoke tenderly to the young woman" — the established formal-translation idiom in English for *dibber 'al-lev*. Notes the same parallel passages. Argues that "tenderly" is the correct English equivalent for the Hebrew wooing-and-comfort idiom.

**PNEUMA's position:** Renders "tried to speak tenderly to her" — the dynamic rendering that captures both the tender content and the pathos of the situation (a rapist attempting tenderness). The word "tried" is PNEUMA's addition, not in the Hebrew, intended to signal the futility or inadequacy of Shechem's gestures.

**SOPHIA's resolution:** The council adopts "spoke tenderly to the young woman" (LOGOS) over EMET's literal rendering and PNEUMA's expanded rendering. EMET's literal form is preserved in the council notes as background. PNEUMA's "tried to" is not in the Hebrew — it imports an interpretive judgment about Shechem's success or sincerity that the text itself does not make; to include it would be to adjudicate between possible responses of Dinah's that the text never gives us. The Hebrew simply says he spoke to her heart — whether those words achieved anything in Dinah is not reported. The council does not invent that silence into a statement.

The full moral weight of the juxtaposition — violation in verse 2, tender wooing in verse 3 — is preserved in the council notes, not dissolved by the choice of "tenderly."

---

### ⚠️ 34:7 — *nevalah be-Yisra'el*: "Disgrace" vs. "Outrage" vs. "Shameful Thing"

**Issue:** The word נְבָלָה (*nevalah*) at the heart of the judicial formula carries a range from "foolishness" (the root *naval* = "to be base, degraded, fool") to "communal-moral outrage" to "disgrace." The question is which English word captures the compound of personal degradation, social violation, and quasi-legal condemnation.

**EMET's position:** Renders "a disgrace in Israel." Notes the root, the legal context, and the recurring formula. Argues "disgrace" is close to the Hebrew without over-specifying.

**LOGOS's position:** Renders "an outrage in Israel." Notes the formula's juridical and communal force: not merely a personal shame but a violation of the community's foundational moral order. Argues "outrage" captures this force.

**PNEUMA's position:** Renders in the verse text "a shameful thing in Israel" but uses "an outrage" in the notes. "Shameful thing" is PNEUMA's dynamic rendering of the verse for narrative pace and clarity.

**SOPHIA's resolution:** The council adopts "an outrage in Israel" (LOGOS). "Disgrace" is accurate but primarily personal in English; "outrage" carries the communal-violation sense that the Hebrew formula demands — an act that outrages the order of the community, not merely one that shames an individual. "Shameful thing" (PNEUMA) is weaker still and loses the juridical weight. The standard English rendering of this formula in scholarship is "outrage" (cf. NRSV, ESV, NJPS). The council adopts it.

---

### ⚠️ 34:25 — *ko'avim* ("In Pain"): Scope of the Verse

**Issue:** The verse specifies three timing elements: (1) "on the third day," (2) "when they were in pain" (*bihyotam ko'avim*), and (3) "while it felt secure" (*betach*). The disagreement is minor — concerning whether to expand the "in pain" phrase.

**EMET's position:** Renders "when they were in pain" — closely literal to the Hebrew participial construction.

**LOGOS's position:** Renders "when they were in pain" — identical.

**PNEUMA's position:** Renders "while they were all still weak with pain" — expanding the Hebrew *ko'avim* with "all still weak with," adding "all" (from the context but not in this phrase) and "weak" (interpretive addition). The expansion is vivid but not in the source.

**SOPHIA's resolution:** The council adopts "when they were in pain" (EMET, LOGOS) — 2 against 1 — over PNEUMA's expanded rendering. "Weak with pain" is accurate as a description of circumcision recovery but is interpretive in a way the Hebrew is not. The Hebrew's *ko'avim* means "being in pain / aching"; the timing-word "when" (*bihyotam*) does the work of connecting the vulnerability to the act. PNEUMA's expansion is better suited to a reading note than to the verse text.

All three translators note, and the council adopts, the full clinical and moral weight: the third day is the day of maximum post-circumcision inflammation and incapacitation; Simeon and Levi chose this window deliberately; the men of Shechem had complied in good faith; they were killed in the very condition their compliance created.

---

### ⚠️ 34:30 — Jacob's Rebuke: The Character of Jacob's Speech

**Issue:** There is no substantive verbal disagreement among the three translators on verse 30 — all render both key idioms similarly. The disagreement is in how the council notes should characterize Jacob's speech.

**EMET's position:** Emphasizes Jacob's conspicuous silences: he does not say "you have done evil," he does not mention Dinah's welfare, he does not grieve for the dead. "His silence on the moral substance of what his sons have done — and his exclusive focus on his own vulnerability — is as much a textual datum as the massacre itself."

**LOGOS's position:** Documents the same observation: "Jacob's complaint is explicitly pragmatic — he fears that the surrounding populations will now combine against his small household and destroy them. He does not say that the massacre was wrong, nor does he express grief for Dinah or for the dead."

**PNEUMA's position:** Extends the observation into a characterological reading: "This is the patriarch who had twenty years to become Israel, who wrestled with God and received a new name, who built an altar at Shechem and called it El-Elohe-Israel — and when his daughter is raped and his sons respond with massacre, his first word is about his own position among the Canaanites." PNEUMA reads Jacob's silence as evidence that he "is still becoming the person his new name demands, and who in this moment falls short of it."

**SOPHIA's resolution:** The council adopts EMET's formulation as the translation note's primary statement — it reports the textual datum without importing a characterological verdict on Jacob's spiritual state. PNEUMA's reading is rich and defensible but exceeds what the text itself gives us; to say Jacob "falls short" of his new name is to make a judgment the narrator does not make. What the narrator gives us is Jacob's speech as it is: first-person, pragmatic, silent on Dinah. The council reports that silence and lets it stand.

---

### ⚠️ 34:31 — *hakezzonah*: "Like a Prostitute" — The Unanswered Question

**Issue:** The chapter's final word is a three-word rhetorical question: הַכְזוֹנָה יַעֲשֶׂה אֶת-אֲחֹתֵנוּ (*hakezzonah ya'aseh et-achotenu*, "Shall he treat our sister like a prostitute?"). The minor verbal disagreements are: (1) EMET's "Shall" vs. LOGOS's "Should" vs. PNEUMA's expanded "Should he have been allowed to"; (2) the MT's singular "he" vs. the LXX's plural "they."

**EMET's position:** Renders "Shall he treat our sister as a prostitute?" — precise, clipped, interrogative force. Uses "as" rather than "like."

**LOGOS's position:** Renders "Should he treat our sister like a prostitute?" — adopts "Should" for the moral-judgment register of the interrogative particle ה- and "like" as the standard English preposition for כְּ. Notes that the LXX pluralizes ("shall they treat") but follows the MT's singular, which specifically indicts Shechem.

**PNEUMA's position:** Renders "Should he have been allowed to treat our sister like a prostitute?" — adds "have been allowed to," which is not in the Hebrew and which soften the brothers' directness into a commentary rather than a challenge.

**SOPHIA's resolution:** The council adopts "Should he treat our sister like a prostitute?" (LOGOS). "Should" registers the moral interrogative force more naturally in contemporary English than EMET's "Shall" (which has an archaic or volitional flavor). PNEUMA's expansion is declined — "have been allowed to" is not in the Hebrew and diffuses the question's rhetorical power. The MT's singular is followed; the LXX's plural is documented above.

The council adopts PNEUMA's characterization of the verse's literary function: the question is not meant to resolve the chapter but to name its permanent tension. Two things are true at once: Shechem committed an outrage, and the brothers committed an outrage. Both are real. Neither cancels the other. The text does not adjudicate. The silence after this question — no divine word, no narrator's verdict, no response from Jacob — is the chapter's most powerful statement. The reader is left where the Hebrew leaves them: holding both truths, without resolution, which is the text's most demanding gift.
